statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
This is the easy version of the problem. The difference between the versions is that in this version the second test is $ n=100\,000 $ . Hacks are not allowed in this problem.

Let's call an integer sequence $ a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n $ distance-convex if $ |a_{i} - a_{i-1}| < |a_{i+1} - a_{i}| $ for every $ 1 < i < n $ . In other words, if you jump through the points $ a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n $ on the number line in this order, each next jump is strictly longer than the previous one.

You are given two integers $ n $ and $ m $ . Find a distance-convex sequence $ a $ of length $ n $ that contains at most $ m $ distinct values. In terms of the jump sequence, that would mean that you need to make $ (n-1) $ jumps of increasing lengths while visiting at most $ m $ distinct points. $ -10^{18} \le a_i \le 10^{18} $ should hold for all $ 1 \le i \le n $ .

Input Format
Input consists of two integers $ n $ and $ m $ on a single line.

There are only 2 tests for this problem.

- $ n = 8 $ , $ m = 6 $ ;
- $ n = 100\,000 $ , $ m = 15\,000 $ .

It is guaranteed that an answer exists for both tests.Hacks are not allowed in this problem.

Output Format
Print a distance-convex sequence $ a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n $ that contains at most $ m $ distinct values. $ -10^{18} \le a_i \le 10^{18} $ should hold for all $ 1 \le i \le n $ .

If there are multiple solutions, print any of them.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
8 6
Output
1 1 3 6 10 3 11 1

Note
The sequence $ [1, 1, 3, 6, 10, 3, 11, 1] $ has length $ n=8 $ and contains $ 5 $ different values, which is less than or equal to $ m=6 $ . The differences between consecutive values are $ 0,2,3,4,7,8,10 $ , a strictly increasing sequence.

$ [1, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 1] $ would be another valid answer.

hints.title: Hint1
hints.content: The expected is $$$n = mlogm - O(m)$$$ steps using harmonic sum.

hints.title: Hint2
hints.content: Think of a solution in 2D (not needed but good intuition).

hints.title: Hint3
hints.content: Put points as an almost regular polygon and keep doing circles around it jumping with step $$$1$$$, then $$$2$$$, then $$$3$$$...

hints.title: Hint4
hints.content: Think how to project this solution to 1D

solutions.title: Solution
solutions.content:
2147I1 - Longest Increasing Path (Easy Version)
We like to motivate the solution in the following way. Consider the problem in 2D. One of the first things one might try is to put the points in an almost regular polygon (a polygon such that each edge is $$$\epsilon$$$-larger than the previous edge) and get a solution $$$n \approx m\log m$$$ by doing loops around the polygon skipping $$$i$$$ points per jump on the $$$i$$$-th loop. It gets about $$$\lfloor n/i \rfloor$$$ jumps on the $$$i$$$-th loop so about $$$m \log m$$$ in total.
So the idea is to
project
the points into the $$$x$$$ axis in a way that each edge is also $$$\epsilon$$$-larger than the previous edge (or at least most of them). There are many ways to do that but one way is to choose some big integer distance $$$C$$$ and add first $$$\frac{m}{2}$$$ points from left to right where adjacent distances will be $$$C+1, C+2, C+3, \ldots C+\frac{m}{2}$$$ and then another $$$\frac{m}{2}$$$ points from right to left such that their distance from the previous added point is $$$C+\frac{m}{2} +1,C+\frac{m}{2} +2, \ldots , C+m$$$.
With this construction, we will do the following algorithm. Fix a step size $$$k = 0$$$. For each step size $$$k$$$ move from left to right skipping $$$k$$$ points each jump and then from right to left skipping $$$k$$$ points per jump until no jumps are possible. Then increase $$$k$$$ by $$$1$$$ and repeat. This gets at least $$$m\log m -3m$$$ jumps.
Proof for mlogm -3m bound (although in practice its like mlogm -1.5m)
For a fixed $$$k$$$, the previous step was $$$k-1$$$ so we are standing at one of the first $$$k-1$$$ points and we will keep jumping until we reach one of the last k positions. That is, $$$\lfloor \frac{\frac{m}{2}-k}{k} \rfloor = \lfloor \frac{m}{2k}\rfloor -1$$$ jumps worst case and $$$\lfloor \frac{m}{2k}\rfloor-0.5$$$ in average. Assume for a moment that we can turn from going right to going left without increasing k. Then each of those terms can be counted twice, giving $$$$$$\sum 2\lfloor \frac{m}{2i}\rfloor - 2m$$$$$$ Finally $$$2\lfloor \frac{x}{2}\rfloor \geq \lfloor x\rfloor-1$$$ (though half the time $$$2\lfloor\frac{x}{2}\rfloor=\lfloor x \rfloor$$$). So bound is $$$\sum \lfloor\frac{m}{k}\rfloor-3m \approx m \log m - 3m$$$ but in average $$$m \log m-1.5m$$$
Let's prove we can turn without having to increase $$$k$$$. Let the last few jumps before turn be the following picture.
Black are jumps of step size $$$k$$$ (skipping $$$k$$$ points not drawn), red distance is the skip we want to perform and letters denote the actual real distance between each jump. So we need to prove $$$b  \lt  r  \lt  f$$$. First, $$$b \lt r$$$ is trivial. Let $$$t \lt k$$$ be the skip distance in points of the last black arc. $$$$$$f-e= \sum_k (D+k+i) - \sum_k(D+i) = k^2$$$$$$ $$$$$$d-c= tk$$$$$$ $$$$$$r= e+d-c = f-k^2 +tk^2 = f - k(k-t)  \lt  f$$$$$$ Hence we can turn back and keep jumping with the same length.
For $$$m = 15000$$$, $$$m\log m \approx 145000$$$ so this solves the easy version with some margin.
